schools.summary: Isaac blesses Jacob and forbids a Canaanite wife: 'and Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him, and said unto him, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan' — Isaac now, deliberately and knowingly (no longer deceived, ch. 27), blessing Jacob and charging him (the patriarch confirming the blessing to Jacob with open eyes, the contrast with the stolen blessing, ch. 27 — here Isaac freely and consciously blessing the heir, the deliberate conveying of the covenant); Isaac, having acquiesced in God's purpose (27:33), now intentionally blessing Jacob (vv.3-4) and charging him (the patriarch ratifying the blessing, the acceptance of God's choice of Jacob); 'Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan' (Isaac charging Jacob not to marry a Canaanite, as Esau had, 26:34-35, to his parents' grief, v.46, the care to keep the covenant-line from the accursed, idolatrous Canaanites, 24:3, the marriage in the faith, the charge that sent Jacob to the kindred, v.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Isaac called Jacob, and blessed him, and charged him, and said unto him, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan
Isaac blessing Jacob and forbidding a Canaanite wife
the deliberate, conscious conveying of the blessing (contrast ch. 27)
keeping the covenant-line from the Canaanites (24:3; 26:34-35)

schools.summary: Isaac sends Jacob to the kindred for a wife: 'arise, go to Padanaram, to the house of Bethuel thy mother's father; and take thee a wife from thence of the daughters of Laban thy mother's brother' — Isaac sending Jacob to Padan-aram (Mesopotamia, the kindred), to the house of Bethuel (Rebekah's father, 24:24, the journey to the covenant family, the seeking of a wife among the kindred, as Abraham for Isaac, ch. 24); Jacob to take a wife 'of the daughters of Laban thy mother's brother' (from the family of Laban, Rebekah's brother, 24:29, the wife of the kindred and faith, the covenant-marriage within the family, as Isaac's, ch. 24, the providence directing Jacob to Rachel, 29:10-11); Isaac's sending Jacob to Padan-aram serving both for a wife and (by Rebekah's design, 27:43-45) for safety from Esau (the providence ordering the exile that would build the house of Israel, chs. 29-30, the journey to the kindred) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Arise, go to Padanaram, to the house of Bethuel thy mother's father; and take thee a wife from thence of the daughters of Laban thy mother's brother
Isaac sending Jacob to the kindred for a wife
the covenant-marriage within the family (24:24, 29), as Isaac's (ch. 24)
the journey providence would overrule for safety and for the house of Israel (chs. 29-30)

schools.summary: Isaac's blessing of fruitfulness in the name of God Almighty: 'and God Almighty bless thee, and make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, that thou mayest be a multitude of people' — Isaac blessing Jacob in the name of God Almighty (El Shaddai, the name by which God had revealed himself to Abraham in the covenant, 17:1, the God of the covenant invoked, the blessing grounded on the Almighty's power to fulfill it, the covenant-name marking the covenant-blessing); 'make thee fruitful, and multiply thee' (the blessing of a numerous posterity, as promised to Abraham, 17:6, the multiplying of Jacob's seed, the covenant-promise of fruitfulness conveyed to the heir); 'that thou mayest be a multitude of people' (Jacob to become a multitude, a company of peoples, the nation of Israel, 35:11, a nation and a company of nations, the covenant-promise of a great people, the blessing of Abraham, 17:4-6, conveyed to Jacob) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And God Almighty bless thee, and make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, that thou mayest be a multitude of people
Isaac's blessing of fruitfulness in the name of God Almighty
God Almighty (El Shaddai, 17:1), the covenant-name invoked
the promise of a numerous posterity and a great people (17:6; 35:11)

schools.summary: The blessing of Abraham conveyed to Jacob: 'and give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed with thee; that thou mayest inherit the land wherein thou art a stranger, which God gave unto Abraham' — Isaac praying God to 'give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed' — the full covenant-blessing made to Abraham (12:2-3; 17:4-8; 22:17-18) now consciously and deliberately conveyed to Jacob (and his seed, Clarke noting that 'the blessing of Abraham, which is to come upon' the heir, is confirmed, the covenant — the land, the seed, and the blessing of all nations, and supremely the Messianic line, the seed in whom all families are blessed, v.14 — bestowed on Jacob as the chosen heir, Isaac, now with open eyes, contrast ch. 27, freely ratifying God's choice, Hebrews 11:21, by faith Isaac blessed Jacob); the blessing including the inheritance of 'the land wherein thou art a stranger' (Canaan, the land of promise, where Jacob, like Abraham, sojourned as a stranger, Hebrews 11:9, 13, the covenant-inheritance of Canaan conveyed to Jacob and his seed, the land promised to Abraham, 13:15; 17:8, now to Jacob); 'which God gave unto Abraham' (the land and blessing given to Abraham, 17:8, now confirmed to Jacob, the covenant continued in the chosen line, narrowed from Abraham through Isaac to Jacob, the blessing of Abraham the heritage of the heir) — Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What is 'the blessing of Abraham' conveyed to Jacob?

sides.position: The full covenant-blessing made to Abraham (12:2-3; 17:4-8; 22:17-18) - the land of Canaan, the numberless seed, and the blessing of all nations in the seed (supremely Christ) - consciously conveyed to Jacob the chosen heir, Isaac ratifying God's choice by faith (Hebrews 11:21)

themes: And give thee the blessing of Abraham, to thee, and to thy seed with thee; that thou mayest inherit the land... which God gave unto Abraham
the blessing of Abraham conveyed to Jacob (the crux of the covenant-blessing)
the full covenant (the land, the seed, the blessing of all nations) consciously conveyed to the chosen heir
Isaac ratifying God's choice by faith (Hebrews 11:21)

schools.summary: Jacob's departure to Padan-aram: 'and Isaac sent away Jacob: and he went to Padanaram unto Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian, the brother of Rebekah, Jacob's and Esau's mother' — Isaac sending Jacob away (with the blessing, vv.3-4, and the charge, vv.1-2), and Jacob setting out for Padan-aram, to Laban (Rebekah's brother, 24:29, the Syrian/Aramean, the departure of the heir, the journey to the kindred, and into exile); Jacob going to Laban (the brother of Rebekah, the family of Nahor, 22:20-23) — the covenant kindred (the place of the wife and the long sojourn, chs. 29-31); 'the brother of Rebekah, Jacob's and Esau's mother' (the careful noting of the family relation, Laban the uncle, the providence sending Jacob to the kindred, the journey that fulfilled both the charge, a wife of the kindred, and the flight, from Esau, 27:43, the exile of the heir) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Isaac sent away Jacob: and he went to Padanaram unto Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian, the brother of Rebekah, Jacob's and Esau's mother
Jacob's departure to Padan-aram
the heir sent with the blessing to the covenant kindred (24:29)
the exile that fulfilled both the charge and the flight (27:43)

schools.summary: Esau observes the blessing and charge given to Jacob: 'when Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob, and sent him away to Padanaram, to take him a wife from thence; and that as he blessed him he gave him a charge, saying, Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan' — Esau observing that Jacob was blessed and sent to the kindred for a wife (the elder noting the favor and the charge, the occasion of his own, misjudged, attempt to please his parents, v.9); Esau seeing the charge — that Jacob was not to marry a Canaanite (v.1, the elder perceiving that the Canaanite marriages were displeasing, the observation that prompted his attempt to mend matters, v.9); Esau, seeing that the Canaanite wives (his own, 26:34) grieved his parents, seeking to please them by another marriage (v.9, the carnal man's misjudged remedy, marrying an Ishmaelite, still outside the covenant-line, the profane Esau trying to win favor by an outward act) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: When Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob, and sent him away to Padanaram... Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan
Esau observing the blessing and charge given to Jacob
the elder perceiving his Canaanite wives grieved his parents (26:34-35)
the occasion of his misjudged attempt to please them (v.9)

schools.summary: Jacob's obedience to his parents: 'and that Jacob obeyed his father and his mother, and was gone to Padanaram' — Esau also observing that Jacob had obeyed his parents (Isaac's charge, v.1, and Rebekah's plan, 27:43) and gone to Padan-aram (the obedience of Jacob, to both father and mother, the contrast with Esau's self-willed marriages, 26:34, the heir's compliance); Jacob's obedience to both parents — the dutiful son going as charged (the contrast with Esau, who pleased himself, 26:34, the obedience that Esau noted, and tried to imitate, v.9); 'was gone to Padanaram' (Jacob already gone to the kindred, v.5, the obedience accomplished, the observation that moved Esau to his own attempt, v.9, the heir on his way) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And that Jacob obeyed his father and his mother, and was gone to Padanaram
Jacob's obedience to his parents
the dutiful heir, in contrast with Esau's self-willed marriages (26:34)
the obedience Esau noted and tried to imitate (v.9)

schools.summary: Esau perceives the Canaanite wives displeased Isaac: 'and Esau seeing that the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac his father' — Esau now perceiving (what he should have seen before) that the Canaanite women (his own wives, 26:34-35) displeased his father (the belated realization, the elder seeing the cause of the grief, 26:35, the occasion of his attempt to mend matters, v.9); the Canaanite wives displeasing Isaac (and Rebekah, 26:35) — the grief of the godly parents (the cause Esau now perceived, the displeasure that prompted his new marriage); Esau's perceiving the displeasure leading him to marry an Ishmaelite (v.9) — yet still outside the covenant-line (Ishmael, not the chosen seed, the carnal man's misjudged remedy, the attempt to please his parents by an outward act, without true understanding of the covenant, the profane Esau's mistaken correction) — Gill.

themes: And Esau seeing that the daughters of Canaan pleased not Isaac his father
Esau perceiving the Canaanite wives displeased Isaac
the belated realization of the cause of his parents' grief (26:35)
the occasion of his misjudged remedy (v.9)

schools.summary: Esau marries an Ishmaelite (the misjudged remedy): 'then went Esau unto Ishmael, and took unto the wives which he had Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael Abraham's son, the sister of Nebajoth, to be his wife' — Esau, to please his parents (vv.6-8), taking a third wife from the family of Ishmael (Abraham's son, 25:12-16, Mahalath, sister of Nebajoth, the attempt to marry within the wider family of Abraham, not a Canaanite, the misjudged remedy); Esau taking Mahalath 'unto the wives which he had' (26:34) — adding a third wife (not putting away the Canaanites, the polygamy, the half-measure that did not truly mend the fault, the Canaanite wives kept); Esau's marrying an Ishmaelite — though within Abraham's wider family — still outside the covenant-line (Ishmael, the son of the bondwoman, cast out, 21:10-12, not the chosen seed, the carnal man's misjudged attempt to please his parents by an outward, half-right act, without understanding the covenant, the chosen line in Isaac and Jacob, not Ishmael, the profane Esau's mistaken correction, the marriage that did not mend matters) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: Then went Esau unto Ishmael, and took unto the wives which he had Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael Abraham's son... to be his wife
Esau marrying an Ishmaelite (the misjudged remedy)
the carnal man's mistaken attempt to please his parents by an outward act
still outside the covenant-line (Ishmael cast out, 21:10-12), the Canaanite wives kept (26:34)

schools.summary: Jacob sets out alone from Beersheba toward Haran: 'and Jacob went out from Beersheba, and went toward Haran' — Jacob setting out from Beersheba (his father's dwelling, 26:23) toward Haran (Padan-aram, the kindred, vv.2, 5, the lone traveler, without retinue, unlike Abraham's servant, ch. 24, the fugitive heir on his long journey, the setting for the vision, vv.11-15); Jacob leaving Beersheba (the place of the covenant and worship, 26:23-25) — going out alone, a stranger and pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13, the heir leaving home and the land of promise, for a time, the lonely departure); 'went toward Haran' (the long journey to the kindred, vv.2, 5, the fugitive, from Esau, 27:43, and pilgrim, the providence ordering the way, and the vision at Bethel, vv.11-15, to comfort and assure the lonely traveler) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob went out from Beersheba, and went toward Haran
Jacob setting out alone from Beersheba toward Haran
the lone traveler and pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13), the fugitive heir
the setting for the vision that would comfort him (vv.11-15)

schools.summary: Jacob lodges with a stone for a pillow: 'and he lighted upon a certain place, and tarried there all night, because the sun was set; and he took of the stones of that place, and put them for his pillows, and lay down in that place to sleep' — Jacob, overtaken by nightfall, lodging at a certain place (later Bethel, v.19, the lone traveler resting in the open, the providence, the 'certain place' where God would meet him, the humble lodging of the heir); Jacob taking stones for his pillow (his head-rest) — the hard lodging of the pilgrim (the heir of the covenant sleeping on the ground with a stone for a pillow, the humble circumstances, the contrast with the glory of the vision to come, vv.12-15); 'lay down in that place to sleep' (Jacob sleeping, the setting for the dream, vv.12-15, the lonely, weary traveler about to be visited by God, the providence preparing the great vision, the place that would become Bethel, v.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And he lighted upon a certain place, and tarried there all night... and he took of the stones of that place, and put them for his pillows, and lay down in that place to sleep
Jacob lodging at the place with a stone for a pillow
the humble lodging of the lone, weary pilgrim
the setting for the dream, the place that would become Bethel (v.19)

schools.summary: The dream of the ladder: 'and he dreamed, and behold a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven: and behold the angels of God ascending and descending on it' — Jacob dreaming of a ladder (or stairway) set on the earth and reaching to heaven, among the many interpretations the chief and consistent being: (a) the providence of God, by which he watches over and governs his people on earth from heaven (the connection between heaven and earth, God's care reaching down to the lonely fugitive); (b) the ministry of angels (the angels ascending and descending) — the communion of heaven and earth, the angels sent forth to minister to the heirs of salvation (Hebrews 1:14), watching over Jacob; and (c), supremely, the type of Christ — the ladder a figure of Christ, in whom heaven and earth, God and man, are joined ('Jesus Christ is the grand connecting medium between heaven and earth, between God and man'); the dominant Christian reading appealing to the Lord's own word: Jesus saying to Nathanael, 'Hereafter ye shall see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of man' (John 1:51) — applying the ladder to himself, Christ the Mediator (1 Timothy 2:5), the way (John 14:6), the union of God and man, by whom alone heaven is opened and communion restored, the ladder thus a gospel-figure of the incarnate Christ, in whom both worlds meet; the angels ascending and descending showing the constant ministry of the holy angels (ascending with the prayers and needs of the saints, descending with God's help and protection, the communion of heaven and earth, the comfort to the lonely Jacob that he was not forsaken, but watched over by God and his angels) — Clarke, Calvin, Gill, John 1:51.

sides.position: The providence of God watching over his people, and the ministry of the angels (the communion of heaven and earth, the comfort to the lonely Jacob)
Supremely, the type of Christ the Mediator, in whom heaven and earth, God and man, are joined - applied by the Lord himself (John 1:51), the union of God and man

themes: And he dreamed, and behold a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven: and behold the angels of God ascending and descending on it
the dream of the ladder (the crux of its signification)
the providence of God and the ministry of the angels (the communion of heaven and earth)
supremely, the type of Christ the Mediator (John 1:51), in whom God and man are joined

schools.summary: The LORD above the ladder renews the covenant of the land: 'and, behold, the LORD stood above it, and said, I am the LORD God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac: the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed' — the LORD (Jehovah) standing above the ladder and revealing himself as the covenant-God of the fathers (the God of Abraham and Isaac, Exodus 3:6, the covenant-God appearing to Jacob, as to Abraham and Isaac, the assurance that the God of his fathers was his God, the renewal of the covenant to the third patriarch); the title — 'the LORD God of Abraham... and the God of Isaac' (the God of the covenant, the fathers' God, the continuity of the covenant, the God who had blessed Abraham and Isaac now confirming the blessing to Jacob); 'the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed' (the LORD renewing the promise of the land, Canaan, where Jacob lay, to him and his seed, 13:15; 17:8; 26:3, the covenant-inheritance confirmed to Jacob, the land of promise given to the heir and his posterity) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And, behold, the LORD stood above it, and said, I am the LORD God of Abraham thy father, and the God of Isaac: the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed
the LORD above the ladder renewing the covenant of the land
the covenant-God of the fathers (Exodus 3:6) appearing to Jacob
the land-promise confirmed to the heir and his seed (13:15; 17:8)

schools.summary: The promise of the seed and the blessing of all families renewed to Jacob: 'and thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt spread abroad to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south: and in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed' — the LORD renewing the promise of a numberless seed (as the dust, 13:16; the stars, 15:5) and the spreading abroad (in every direction) of Jacob's posterity (the covenant-promise of multiplication and expansion, Israel to fill the land and spread); the great gospel promise — 'in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed' (12:3; 22:18; 26:4, the blessing of all families/nations in Jacob's seed, Paul declaring the seed to be Christ, Galatians 3:16, in whom all nations are blessed, the covenant of grace, the gospel, confirmed to Jacob); the seed, the spreading, and the blessing of all families — the full covenant-promise (made to Abraham and Isaac, 12:3; 26:4) renewed entire to Jacob (the line of the Messiah, the seed, Christ, confirmed in him, the gospel-promise the heritage of the heir) — Gill, Clarke, Wesley.

themes: And thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth... and in thee and in thy seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed
the promise of the seed and the blessing of all families renewed to Jacob
the numberless, spreading seed (13:16) and the gospel promise (12:3; 26:4)
the line of the Messiah (the seed, Christ, Galatians 3:16) confirmed in Jacob

schools.summary: The LORD's personal promise of presence and keeping to Jacob: 'and, behold, I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest, and will bring thee again into this land; for I will not leave thee, until I have done that which I have spoken to thee of' — the LORD giving Jacob the great personal promise of his presence ('I am with thee') and keeping (protection in all his journeys, the comfort to the lonely fugitive, vv.10-11, the covenant-presence, as to Isaac, 26:24, the assurance of God's protecting care wherever he went); 'will bring thee again into this land' (the assurance that Jacob, though now leaving, into exile, would be brought back to the land of promise, the comfort of a safe return, fulfilled, ch. 33; 35, the providence guaranteeing the homecoming); 'I will not leave thee, until I have done that which I have spoken to thee of' (God's faithful promise never to forsake Jacob until all was fulfilled, the perseverance of God's care, the assurance that God would perform all his word, the great comfort, Hebrews 13:5, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee, the covenant-faithfulness to the heir) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And, behold, I am with thee, and will keep thee in all places whither thou goest, and will bring thee again into this land; for I will not leave thee, until I have done that which I have spoken to thee of
the LORD's personal promise of presence and keeping to Jacob
the great promise of presence, protection, and safe return (26:24; ch. 35)
the covenant-faithfulness - I will not leave thee till I have done all (Hebrews 13:5)

schools.summary: Jacob awakens to the sense of God's presence: 'and Jacob awaked out of his sleep, and he said, Surely the LORD is in this place; and I knew it not' — Jacob, waking from the dream (vv.12-15), realizing that the LORD was present in that place (where he had met him, the sense of God's special presence, the awe of the divine manifestation, the recognition that God was there); Jacob's 'and I knew it not' (he had not known, when he lay down, a common place, a stone for a pillow, v.11, that God would meet him there, the unexpected manifestation, the lesson that God is present where we know it not, the wonder at the unlooked-for visitation); Jacob's 'Surely the LORD is in this place' expressing the sense of God's special, manifest presence (not that God is absent elsewhere, but that he had here revealed himself, the awe, the recognition that turned the common place into the house of God, v.17, the comfort and wonder of the visited fugitive) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Jacob awaked out of his sleep, and he said, Surely the LORD is in this place; and I knew it not
Jacob's awakening to the sense of God's presence
the unlooked-for manifestation in the common place where he lay (v.11)
the lesson that God is present where we know it not

schools.summary: Jacob's awe - the house of God and the gate of heaven: 'and he was afraid, and said, How dreadful is this place! this is none other but the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven' — Jacob 'was afraid' — filled with an awe of the greatness and glory of God (the reverent fear that the manifestation of God's presence produces, Exodus 3:5-6, the holy dread, not slavish terror, 'How dreadful, awe-inspiring, to be reverenced, is this place!' — the place made awful by God's presence, the reverent fear of the visited fugitive); Jacob calling the place 'the house of God (Bethel, v.19)' — the place where God had manifested himself, a house/dwelling of God (not that God dwells in a place, but that here he had revealed his presence, the place of God's special manifestation, the sanctuary-sense, the place hallowed by God's presence); 'this is the gate of heaven' (the place a gate/door of heaven, where heaven seemed opened, the ladder reaching up, vv.12, the angels passing, the communion of heaven and earth opened, the figure of the place of God's manifestation and of access to heaven, and, by some, pointing to Christ, the true gate, John 1:51; 10:9, the awe at the opened heaven) — Clarke, Calvin, Gill, Keil and Delitzsch.

debates.question: What is the sense of 'the house of God... the gate of heaven'?

sides.position: The place hallowed by God's special manifestation (Bethel, v.19), the gate where heaven was opened (the ladder, the angels) - the holy fear at God's manifest presence (Exodus 3:5-6)
A figure, by some, of Christ - the true gate of heaven, the only access to God (John 1:51; 10:9)

themes: And he was afraid, and said, How dreadful is this place! this is none other but the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven
Jacob's awe - the house of God and the gate of heaven (the crux)
the holy fear at God's manifest presence (Exodus 3:5-6), the place hallowed (Bethel, v.19)
the gate where heaven was opened, pointing, by some, to Christ the true gate (John 1:51)

schools.summary: Jacob sets up and anoints the pillar: 'and Jacob rose up early in the morning, and took the stone that he had put for his pillows, and set it up for a pillar, and poured oil upon the top of it' — Jacob, early, taking the stone of his pillow (v.11) and setting it up as a pillar (a memorial, a standing monument of the vision and the place, the marking of the sacred spot, the commemoration of God's appearing); Jacob 'poured oil upon the top of it' — anointing/consecrating the pillar (a token of dedication to God, setting it apart as sacred, the consecration of the memorial, the act of worship and dedication, the oil a sign of consecration, as later the anointing of holy things, the hallowing of the spot); the pillar and the oil an act of grateful worship and dedication — Jacob consecrating the place of God's appearing (the memorial of the covenant-vision, the spot marked and hallowed, Bethel, v.19, the patriarch's response of worship to the manifestation) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Wesley.

themes: And Jacob rose up early in the morning, and took the stone that he had put for his pillows, and set it up for a pillar, and poured oil upon the top of it
Jacob setting up and anointing the pillar
the memorial of the vision and the consecration of the sacred spot
the patriarch's response of grateful worship to the manifestation

schools.summary: The naming of Bethel: 'and he called the name of that place Bethel: but the name of that city was called Luz at the first' — Jacob naming the place Bethel ('house of God,' v.17, the name commemorating God's appearing and the sacredness of the spot, the memorial of the vision, the place that would be notable in Israel's history, 35:1-15, later a sanctuary, and sadly a place of idolatry, 1 Kings 12:29); 'Bethel' meaning 'house of God' (v.17, the name fitting the place of God's manifestation, the memorial of the visitation, the hallowed spot named for God's presence); 'the name of that city was called Luz at the first' (the place, or the nearby city, formerly called Luz, almond, or hazel, Jacob renaming it Bethel, the change of name marking the new significance, the house of God, the providence hallowing the once-common place) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And he called the name of that place Bethel: but the name of that city was called Luz at the first
the naming of Bethel
Bethel ('house of God,' v.17), the memorial of God's appearing
the once-common place (Luz) hallowed and renamed, notable in Israel's history (35:1-15)

schools.summary: Jacob's vow: 'and Jacob vowed a vow, saying, If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way that I go, and will give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on' — 'Jacob vowed a vow' the first vow recorded in Scripture (a vow being a solemn, voluntary promise to God, made, Clarke notes, with prayer, the patriarch binding himself to God, the response of faith and devotion to the vision, vv.12-15); Jacob's vow framed conditionally — 'If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way... and will give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on' (and bring me home, v.21), the crux: is this a low, mercenary bargain (Jacob haggling with God, making his service conditional on benefits), or the language of faith? the dominant reading holding it not a bargaining condition but the laying hold of God's own promise: the 'if' echoing the very things God had just promised (I am with thee, will keep thee, will bring thee again, v.15) — Jacob taking God at his word, vowing to respond if (as he believes) God does what he has said, the vow of faith, asking only necessities (bread and raiment, cf. 1 Timothy 6:8) and pledging devotion, not a doubting or grasping condition, but the modest, believing response of the pilgrim to the promise; Jacob asking only the necessities — bread and raiment (the content of godly contentment, 1 Timothy 6:8; Matthew 6:25-33, the moderation of his desire, the vow of one content with God's provision, the believing, humble petition) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: Is Jacob's conditional vow a mercenary bargain or the language of faith?

sides.position: The language of faith - Jacob laying hold of God's own just-given promise (v.15, I am with thee, will keep thee, will bring thee again), asking only necessities (1 Timothy 6:8), pledging devotion; the modest, believing response of the pilgrim
Read by some as a low, conditional bargain - Jacob making his service contingent on benefits (a view the witnesses largely reject)

themes: And Jacob vowed a vow, saying, If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way that I go, and will give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on
Jacob's vow (the crux of its character)
the conditional 'if' as the language of faith, laying hold of God's own promise (v.15) - not a mercenary bargain
asking only necessities, bread and raiment (1 Timothy 6:8), the first vow in Scripture

schools.summary: Jacob's vow that the LORD shall be his God: 'so that I come again to my father's house in peace; then shall the LORD be my God' — the last of the conditions Jacob named: a safe return to his father's house (echoing God's promise, v.15, I will bring thee again into this land, the desire for a peaceful homecoming, the laying hold of God's word, the pilgrim's hope of return); 'then shall the LORD be my God' Jacob's vow to take the LORD (Jehovah, the covenant-God who had appeared, v.13) as his God (not that he would only then begin to worship God, he already feared God, but a solemn, personal, covenantal taking of the LORD as his own God, the appropriating of the covenant-God by faith, the personal commitment of the pilgrim, the response of the heart to God's promise, the LORD his God, in covenant, Exodus 6:7); Jacob's 'then shall the LORD be my God' the language of personal, covenantal faith — taking God at his word (v.15) and binding himself to him (not a mercenary condition but the believing appropriation of the covenant-God, the personal devotion of the heir) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: So that I come again to my father's house in peace; then shall the LORD be my God
Jacob's vow that the LORD shall be his God
the safe return echoing God's promise (v.15)
the personal, covenantal taking of the LORD as his own God (Exodus 6:7)

schools.summary: Jacob's vow of the pillar and the tithe: 'and this stone, which I have set for a pillar, shall be God's house: and of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth unto thee' — Jacob vowing that the pillar (and the place, Bethel) should be a house of God (a place of worship, the dedication of the spot to God's service, fulfilled when Jacob returned and built an altar there, 35:7, the consecration of the place, the memorial to become a sanctuary); Jacob vowing 'I will surely give the tenth unto thee' — to give God a tenth (tithe) of all that God gave him (the dedication of a portion to God, as Abraham gave tithes to Melchizedek, 14:20, the acknowledgment that all is of God, and the returning of a portion in worship and gratitude, the pledge of devotion and thankful giving); Jacob's vow (the pillar a house of God, and the tithe) the response of grateful faith — the dedication of the place and a portion of his substance to God (the worship and thankfulness of the pilgrim, the binding of himself to God's service, the proper, devout vow, not a bargain, but a grateful pledge) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And this stone, which I have set for a pillar, shall be God's house: and of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth unto thee
Jacob's vow of the pillar and the tithe
the dedication of the place (Bethel, fulfilled 35:7) as a house of God
the tithe vowed (as Abraham, 14:20), the grateful devotion of faith
